coredata.dc:title: Efficient multi-component PL emission tunning in single emissive layer (SEL) by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots in type-II ZnSe/af-GQDs soft core/shell interface as an efficient phosphor
coredata.dc:creator: Mustafa, Faiza
Aslam, Samia
Arshad, Asim
Ahmad, Ashfaq
Sagar, Sadia
Yun, Sining
coredata.dc:description: This research demonstrates first report on successful formation of ZnSe/amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs), type-II core-shell structures. The structures having potential as a single emissive layer (SEL), were synthesized via a facile and eco-friendly hot injection method. We achieved a highly efficient tuning of PL emission spectral components in visible region ranging from (∼458 to 750nm). The functional groups attached with af-GQDs rendered soft shell feature to the structures and the soft core-shell interface significantly passivated the strain-induced defect states and reduced Auger recombination (AR). The PL emission properties of ZnSe/af-GQDs were studied through steady state and temperature dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectroscopy within the temperature range (80-300k), using 457nm excitation laser. Three samples including bare ZnSe QDs, bare af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs core/shell structures were prepared. The steady state PL emission spectra revealed a weak blue and pronounced green, yellow, orange and red emission, whereas the full width half maximum (FWHM) of PL spectra distinctly decreased in ZnSe/af-GQDs. The carrier activation and escape energies of radiative and non-radiative recombination respectively were estimated through Arhenious and Brethelot fitting functions for TDPL measurements. The calculated values clearly indicated a decrease in activation energies and an increase in escape energies in ZnSe/af-GQD X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) measurements revealed the presence of Zn 2p and Se (3d) O1s and NIs orbitals of constituent elements including Zn, Se, C, O and N. Tight binding model was applied to estimate the valance and conduction band energies that suggested formation of type-II core-shell band alignment in the structures. The relative PLQY was estimated using comparative method and Rhodamine-B was used as reference and calculated PLQY are 58%, 61% and 83% for ZnSe, af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs. A mechanism of formation of a soft core-shell interface for ZnSe/af-GQDs is clarified. We suggest that ZnSe-/af-GQDs structures synthesized in this report have a great potential for LEDs and display devices in which multi-color components are of paramount importance, such as white light emission. This report may open a gate way for more and vivid possibilities to apply af-GQDs for optoelectronic devices.

head.ce:title: Efficient multi-component PL emission tunning in single emissive layer (SEL) by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots in type-II ZnSe/af-GQDs soft core/shell interface as an efficient phosphor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research demonstrates first report on successful formation of ZnSe/amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs), type-II core-shell structures. The structures having potential as a single emissive layer (SEL), were synthesized via a facile and eco-friendly hot injection method. We achieved a highly efficient tuning of PL emission spectral components in visible region ranging from (∼458 to 750nm). The functional groups attached with af-GQDs rendered soft shell feature to the structures and the soft core-shell interface significantly passivated the strain-induced defect states and reduced Auger recombination (AR). The PL emission properties of ZnSe/af-GQDs were studied through steady state and temperature dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectroscopy within the temperature range (80-300k), using 457nm excitation laser. Three samples including bare ZnSe QDs, bare af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs core/shell structures were prepared. The steady state PL emission spectra revealed a weak blue and pronounced green, yellow, orange and red emission, whereas the full width half maximum (FWHM) of PL spectra distinctly decreased in ZnSe/af-GQDs. The carrier activation and escape energies of radiative and non-radiative recombination respectively were estimated through Arhenious and Brethelot fitting functions for TDPL measurements. The calculated values clearly indicated a decrease in activation energies and an increase in escape energies in ZnSe/af-GQD X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) measurements revealed the presence of Zn 2p and Se (3d) O1s and NIs orbitals of constituent elements including Zn, Se, C, O and N. Tight binding model was applied to estimate the valance and conduction band energies that suggested formation of type-II core-shell band alignment in the structures. The relative PLQY was estimated using comparative method and Rhodamine-B was used as reference and calculated PLQY are 58%, 61% and 83% for ZnSe, af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs. A mechanism of formation of a soft core-shell interface for ZnSe/af-GQDs is clarified. We suggest that ZnSe-/af-GQDs structures synthesized in this report have a great potential for LEDs and display devices in which multi-color components are of paramount importance, such as white light emission. This report may open a gate way for more and vivid possibilities to apply af-GQDs for optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years ZnSe based type-II core-shell quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as promising candidates for renewable light emitting applications such as quantum dot QLEDs and display devices. This is due to their highly tunable band gap in visible region(2.7eV), less resistivity, photostability and longer photoluminescence lifetime [1,2]. Many researchers have recently reported ZnSe based type-II core/shell QDs such as ZnSe/ZnS/CdS, ZnSe/CdS/ZnS, CdxZn1−xS/ZnSe/ZnS, (ZnSe/CdS)CdS for QLED applications [1–4]. Primarily, stabilizing ligands such as trioctylphosphine (TOP), trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO) and tributylphosphine (TBP) were employed to synthesize ZnSe QDs.Whereas TOP and TOPO were also used a solvents in many of the ZnSe synthesis procedures [5,6]. Therefore, ZnSe based luminescent materials involve rigorous synthetic procedures and complex precursors, moreover most of the structures involve cadmium, which is highly toxic. Laterally, researchers formulated eco-friendly and simpler synthesis of ZnSe based core shell structures. Byambasuren et al. reported phosphine free ecofriendly ZnSe based core shell structures. However, to enhance the PL spectral distribution for white light emission, they combined the structures with Zn-Cu-In-S/ZnS core-shell QDs which increased the steps of synthesis [7]. Jin et al. reported the synthesis of five different sized CdZnSe/ZnSe/ZnS structures to obtain tuning of PL spectral components to make the white light emission possible. Although they achieved PL quantum yield of 82% and 91% CRI, however the five different sized layers involved sperate synthesis and robust steps with cadmium precursors [8]. In some earlier reports the PL emission within (630−1100 nm) was obtained by synthesizing InP/ ZnSe QDs with Cu doping and Wei et al. [9] first time obtained PL tuning of metal doped InP/ZnS based structures in visible region (500−600nm). Li et al. [10] reported gallium, copper selenium based ZnSe QDs and obtained multicomponent tunable PL emission in the visible region.whereas the In another recent report, Zhang et al. reported synthesis of Mn: ZnSe QDs and Cu, Mn: ZnSe/ZnS in which metal ion doping was additionally employed to enhance PL emission spectral components to generate white light emission whereas they achieved tunable photoluminescence in (350−650nm) range in visible region [11]. This suggests that the tuning of spectral components with a red shift in visible region only by employing ZnSe/ZnS core-shell structures could not be achieved. Wei et al. also reported similar but a more complex strategy and employed quaternary Ag-Cu-Ga-Se/ZnSe QDs to achieve a tunable photoluminescence between (510 and 620nm) useful for white light emission, featuring green, yellow and orange emission whereas blue and red emission components were missing [12]. Moreover, most of the reports suggest formation of type-I band alignment in core-shell QDs, in which lattice mismatch factor is critically important and limit the choice of shell material to avoid strain induced defects and non-radiative recombination centers. The lattice mis-match factor crucially affects the CRI and PL quantum yield of the structures and poses a challenge in materializing efficient emissive materials.

Another approach for tuning of PL emission in visible region is to design type-II core/shell QDs, in which spectral distribution can be enhanced to obtained desired amount of necessary multi color components suitable for applications such as white light emission. The lattice mismatch between the core and shell develops a synergetic effect of stress and strain at the interface that gives rise to type-II staggered band alignment of core/shell QDs[13]. The staggered band alignment can cause the prominent spectral shift in the absorbance and luminescence spectra of the structures [14]. However, the strain related defects also promote non-radiative recombination referred to as strain induced Auger recombination (AR). This is because, the defects act as hole trap states which boost non-radiative carrier relaxation process. Consequently, PL quantum yield is compromised as the photoluminescence is quenched through variety of routes provided by strain induced defect states [15,16]. Many reports suggest that nonradiative AR can be suppressed by replacing the abrupt hard interface by graded soft interface in core/shell QDs. Currently, graded core/shell QDs, alloyed graded core/shell and giant core /shell with thick shell are the recently reported configurations to develop a soft interface between core and shell [17]. However, the underlying atomistic processes that lead to gradient creation, cation and anion diffusion are still unknown. Moreover, a careful selection of material to create the slope of gradient and sophisticated synthesis procedures are highly desired for development of these smooth interfaces [18]. Hence, more advanced and simpler heterostructures enabling suppressed AR are needed to be explored. Recently, carbon-based materials have attracted tremendous attention as a new class of luminescent materials due to their various advantages such as low cost, easy and green synthesis method, superior photo stability and strong luminescence [19]. Currently huge interest has been shown by research community in carbon- based zero dimensional (0D) nanostructures referred to as graphene quantum dots GQDs. This is due to their unique quantum confinement, edge effects. Ideally, GQDs have single layer of carbon atoms but mostly prepared GQDs have multiple atomic layers and possess oxygen and hydrogen functional groups [20]. In GQDs, the π electrons have a considerable delocalization impact due to graphene's massive planar conjugate structure, and tunable band gap. GQDs exhibit distinct photoluminescence (PL) features, such as strong photostability and adjustable emission wavelength. At presents, research community taped multiple pathways to tune the luminescent properties of the GQDs that involves the size engineering, degree of oxidation and surface functionalization [21]. The functional groups can significantly tailor the luminescent properties of GQDs via quantum confinement effect of conjugate π domain that results in discrete energy levels and edge effects [22]. Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) have been presented as good light emitters with high luminance quantum yield and broad photoluminescence tunability. The af-GQDs shows high electron donating ability due to its edge terminated by the primary amine group that alter the electron cloud distribution of af-GQDs. High quantum efficiency and optical tunability are based on the strong orbital resonance of amino groups with a graphene core [23]. Although, in functionalized GQDs the origin of tunable emission spectra is yet to be fully understood. However, the quantum confinement impact of conjugated π domains, the surface/edge state in GQDs and the distribution of functional groups offer a synergistic impact to dictate the major PL mechanism of GQDs [24]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no research report to-date, to demonstrate employment of af-GQDs in type-II core-shell QDs for light emitting diodes (LEDs) and display devices. The promising properties of af-GQDs with built in graded structure due to its functional groups make them a plausible choice as a soft-shell material to develop soft interface for heterostructure core/shell QDs.

In generating white light emission, the red (R) and Green (G) and (Blue) and yellow (Y) emissions are of vital importance. Martials based on rare earth metal ion and GaN phosphors organic materials, quantum dots, perovskite materials have been widely investigated as emissive layers with tuned PL spectral components in phosphor based WLEDs, organic LEDs (OLEDs), quantum dot LEDs (QLEDs), perovskite LEDs (PeLEDs) [25]. The low color rendering index (CRI) and lack of red color component in phosphor based white LEDs (WLEDs), broad asymmetric emission spectrum and need of expensive organic materials for stacking in White organic (WOLEDs), size dependent nonradiative augur recombination (AR) in white quantum dot LEDs (WQLEDs), toxicity and instability of lead halide perovskite materials in white perovskite LEDs (WPeLEDs,) thus limit the use of these materials for commercial lighting [26–29]. Moreover, to design a material with a wide scope of utilization in LED applications, requires a fine tuning of their PL emission properties with multi-spectral components and investigating simpler and eco-friendly synthesis approach. However, in place of developing multicomponent emissive layers with unwanted molecular interactions, a single emissive layer (SEL), type-II core-shell structure with multi-component PL emission is a preferable choice but has been scarcely reported and the underlying emission mechanisms are unknown [30]. Therefore, there is a strong need to develop a cost effective and stable single layered core/shell emissive material with simple structure for feasible and efficient lighting.

In this paper, we report for the first time, a facile hot injection approach to synthesize the eco-friendly ZnSe/af-GQDs as an efficient single emissive layer (SEL) suitable for LED applications and multi-components emission spectra potentially suitable for white light emission. The SEL was achieved by coating af-GQDs as the soft-shell material to passivate the defect states and to tune the emission properties of ZnSe core. We demonstrate that the attached functional groups reduce the strain induced interfacial defects with natural energy gradient in af-GQDs. We estimated the valance and conduction band positions using tight binding model and found that the ZnSe/af-GQDs form a staggered band alignment of type-II core/shell QDs. We found that the amino functional groups greatly helped in efficient tuning of basic blue, green, yellow and red luminescent centers. Herein, we also clarify the possible mechanism of formation of type-II staggered band alignment within the structures. Temperature dependent PL measurements were conducted to elucidate the PL emission mechanism of the structures.

Zinc nitrate hydrate (99.99%), selenium powder (99.99%), CTAB (99.99%) and glucose (99.99%) were purchased from SIGMA-ALDRICH and used without further purification. Ethylene glycol and hydrazine monohydrate were purchased from DUKSAN and ammonia solution (32%) was purchased from AppliChem.

Zinc selenide (ZnSe) quantum dots (QDs) were synthesized by modifying a simple solvothermal approach [31]. In a typical synthesis, 100 ml solution of deionized water, ethylene glycol and hydrazine hydrate in ratio 6:3:1 were mixed and 50mg CTAB was added under vigorous stirring to transform into a homogenous solution. Later the solution was divided into 80ml and 20ml volumetric portions in separate beakers to obtain zinc and selenium precursor solutions. Then 600 mg zinc acetate Zn(CH3CO2)2 was added into 80 ml solution whereas 400 mg selenium powder was added into 20 ml solution. Then both solutions were mixed and transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and heated for 8 h at 140°C to obtain fine precipitates of ZnSe, these precipitates were later centrifuged and washed with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 50°C in a furnace for 6 h duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino functionalize graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The af-GQDs was prepared using glucose as shown in the schematic representation of Fig. 1 . For the preparation of af-GQDs, 4g of glucose was added into a beaker and heated to 250°C using a hot plate. About 5 min later, the glucose was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to yellow, and then orange in 20 min. The obtained orange colored liquid was added drop by drop into 50mL of 32% ammonia solution, under vigorous stirring to obtain GQDs . Then the solution was heated at 70°C for 3 h until the odor of ammonia disappeared and the solution neutralized to pH 7.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnSe-GQDs core-shell QDs via hot injection method
ce:section.ce:para: The QD dispersions obtained via synthesis of separate QDs was poured into each other via three neck flux under high temperature conditions. The precipitates thus obtained were collected via centrifugation, washing with different solvents followed by drying. The final products were later preserved in dispersions or coated onto a FTO substrate for further characterization.

The crystal structure of the ZnSe/af-GQDs was obtained using Smartlab. The grain size of the QDs were estimated by WH-plots. The vibrational modes of the QDs were investigated using In-Via Raman-PL setup (RENISHAW UK) with 457nm excitation wavelength Ar. laser. The chemical composition of the samples was studied using ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of the QDs were obtained using 2010F-UHR-JEOL-Tokyo, Japan. The absorption spectra of the QDs were recorded using a UV-VIS/NIR spectrometer LAMBDA750(Perkin Elmer) within the spectral range (200−800 nm). The valance and conduction band positions of the core/shell QDs were estimated using linear equation of tight binding model. The temperature dependent Photoluminescence spectra (TDPL) of the samples were recorded within the temperature range 80K−300K using In-Via Raman- PL setup (RENISHAW UK) with LINKAM temperature controller under 457nm excitation Ar. Laser.

The X-ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesized quantum dots are shown in the Fig. 2 (a-c). The XRD pattern of af-GQDs shows two wide peaks centered around 28.5o and 41.8o are corresponding to the (100) and (102) hexagonal planes of carbon network [32,33]. The broad peaks attributed to the nanoscale size of the synthesized af-GQDs with few layers of graphene [34]. For af-GQDs, the lattice distance is determined to be 0.32 nm, which is near to the graphene bulk distance (0.334nm). The fact that oxygen-containing groups between graphene layers are partially eliminated following amino functionalization. The value of d-spacing is in well agreement with the reported literature and might be linked to af-GQDs size and edge effects [34,35].

The XRD peaks of ZnSe are indexed at (100), (220), (200), (004) and (014) that correspond to the hexagonal ZnSe JCPD card No. 96-900-8880 and space group P-43mc (186) with cell parameters a= 3.98000 Å c= 6.53000 Å. Which is in well agreement with already reported literature [36–38]. Some additional peaks also appeared due to the unreacted selenium and impurities that may be attributed to the formation of zinc and selenium complexes such as ZnSeOx-H2O and SeO during reaction [34,35]. The XRD pattern of ZnSe/af-GQDs(core-shell) QDs are indexed at (100), (101), (102), (220), (004), (014), and (400) that correspond to the overlapping hexagonal planes of carbon network in af-GQDs shell and hexagonal core of ZnSe. In addition to the ZnSe/af-GQDs peaks two extra peaks due to the formation of ZnO are indexed at (112) and (201) [39].

In order to estimate the disorder in the structures, strain analysis of the samples was performed using by Williamson Hall (WH) method using Williamson Hall equation of straight line [40].

Equation (1) is the equation of straight line the value of the micro strain “ε” is the slope and “kλ/D” is the y intercept of the straight line, where k is 0.9 (constant) and λ is the wavelength of the CuKα X-Ray radiation. The grain size was also calculated using this model. The WH-plots of the samples are shown in the Fig. 2d–f. The grain sizes and micro strains that are estimated from the linear fit of WH plots and are listed in Table 1 . The size of the bare GQDs (⁓2nm) and ZnSe QDs (8nm) is less than the size of the ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell (25nm). The increase in size in core-shell QDs is due the formation of the af-GQDs shell around ZnSe Core. The micro strain (Table 1) that represents the crystal defects, is negative for CTAB capped ZnSe QDs and af-GQDs that is attributed to the compressional strain due to the quantum size effect. The micro strain for ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell is positive that is ascribed to the tensile strain induced due to the lattice mismatched epitaxially grown af-GQDs shell [41]. The strain that arises due to the structural defects, plays a critical role in tuning the optical properties in core-shell configuration of QDs. The core and the shell strain each other synergistically and can alter their respective properties. Moreover, they also dictate the formation of type-I or type-II band alignment. A tensile strain in a core/shell QD structure indicates formation of type-II core-shell structure [42]. The micro strain for ZnSe/af-GQDs reveals the formation of strain relaxing crystalline defects that attributed to the coverage of defects states of ZnSe by the af-GQDs.

Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of each of three QDs using excitation wave length of 457nm. The Raman spectra of af-GQDs Fig. 3a shows the intense D and G bands

The A1g mode associated with the sp3 hybridization and defected carbon while the E2g mode associated with the sp2 hybridization of graphitic structure [43–45]. The ratio of intensities ID/IG of D and G band (Table 1) confirmed that the functional groups embedded into the graphitic structure [45]. The ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell structure, the Raman spectrum Fig. 2c shows the characteristic peaks of ZnSe and af-GQDs the ID/IG is observed to decrease in the core/shell structure that indicates a decrease in the defects due to shell formation. This also validates the results obtained from XRD.

For the further insight into the intrinsic structure of all samples, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis is performed. In Fig.Sa (see supporting information), TEM image of af-GQDS depicts that largely hollow morphology is attained which is beneficial for the shelling of metallic ZnSe. Fig.Sb shows TEM image of as synthesized ZnSe quantum dots (QDs) which indicated partially spherical morphology along with agglomerated ZnSe QDs as well. Fig. 3d shows as synthesized ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell structure with dark ZnSe core shelled with af-GQDs [47]. Inset in Fig. 3d shows that darker spots of ZnSe cores are visibly encapsulated by af-GQDs. The histogram of the particle size distribution of the ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell (inset) Fig. 3d shows that the size of the core-shell structure is ⁓ 24nm which is in well agreement with the particle size calculated from XRD. This result manifests the successful synthesis of novel ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell quantum dots.

XPS analysis was performed to gain an insight into the chemical compositions of the synthesized nanostructures. Fig. 4 a shows survey spectra of all samples, which confirms the presence of Zn, Se, C, O and N. The survey spectrum of ZnSe shows a low intensity peak of C for ZnSe, assigned to the residual content of CTAB used as capping agent in our samples [48].

High resolution XPS spectra provided a view of chemical commposition of all samples Fig. 4(d−f). Survey spectras in Fig. 4a shows that each sample contains all major consituents e.g. Zn, Se, C, N, and O respectively. Fig. 4b represents that Zn2p in ZnSe exhibits two high intensity peaks around 1028.5 eV and 1042.6 eV, while ZnSe-GQDs shows peaks at 1029.1 eV and 1043.2 eV, which corresponds to Zn2p 5/2 and Zn2 p1/2 respectively. Similarly, Se3d in ZnSe shows two broad peaks at 53.63 eV 54.72 eV, and ZnSe/af-GQDs exhibits peaks at 53.78 eV and 54.85 eV refers to Se3d 1/2 and Se3d 3/2 respectively as shown in Fig. 4c. A slight shift in Zn2p and Se3d peaks after the core-shelling with af-GQDs is observed which occurred due to the attachment of additional functional groups such as O and N as previous literatures reported [49]. Moreover, the XRD and XPS survey spectra in Fig. 4 also provide strong evidence that core-shelling of ZnSe/af-GQDs are amino functionalized which dictated the tuning of emission properties of the core-shell structures and limited non-radiative recombination.

Fig. 4d shows deconvoluted C1s spectra of all samples where peaks at 284.75 eV, 285.45 eV and 287.20 eV represent C-C, C-N and C-O bonds respectively in ZnSe/af-GQDs. Similarly, peaks centered at 284.75 eV, 286.20 eV and 287.85 eV represent C-C, C-N and C-O bonds respectively in GQDs, while peaks at 284.75 eV, 285.45 eV and 287.20 eV represent C-C, C-N and C-O bonds respectively in ZnSe. Low intensity carbon peaks in ZnSe are due to the residual content of CTAB present in the samples. C1s peaks in Fig. 4d show that the highly accumulated C-C bonds are formed in core-shelled ZnSe/af-GQDs similar to af-GQDs C1s peak, moreover, C-N and C-O signals represents a successful chemical integration of functionalized species which are beneficial for introduction of sub-energy states, as reported in literature [50,51].

N1s in Fig. 4e after deconvolution, represents the peaks at 397.2 eV, 399 eV, and 397.5 eV corresponding C-N=C bonds while peak centers at 399.8 eV, 400.5 eV and 398.8 eV represent N-CH2 bonds and the peak centers at 401.6 eV, 401.5 eV, 401.6 eV shows the presence of N-CH3 bonds in ZnSe, af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs, respectively. C-N=C depicts doped nitrogen atoms in the GQDs structure whereas highly accumulated N-CH2, N-CH3 bonds are corresponding to amino functional groups attached to af-GQDs. Literature reports suggest that the amino functionalization mainly provides intermediate energy levels between valance band and conduction band, which can be further utilized to tune the band gap [49–51].

Deconvoluted O1s spectrums are also represented in Fig. 4f which shows high intensity peaks at 530.35 eV, 530.85 eV and 532.20 eV corresponds to metal- oxide (M-O), N-O and C-O bonds in ZnSe/af-GQDs. Similarly, O1s peaks at 530.25 eV, 531.55 eV and 532.65eV belongs to af-GQDs, while peak centers at 530.20 eV, 531.23 eV and 532.51 eV belongs to ZnSe. Highly accumulated Metal-Oxide (M-O) bonds are observed, whereas N-O and C-O bonds represents that functionalized species are largely present in both af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs samples. The energies of the deconvoluted XPS peaks of Zn, Se, C1s, O1s, and N1s are listed in Table S1 (See Supporting information). XPS results provides experimental evidence of the successful synthesis of core/shell structure of ZnSe core with af-GQDs shell.

The structural analysis of the QDs confirms the formation of ZnSe/af-GQDs core- shell structure and also confirmed the presence of functional groups in both af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs. It was observed from the structural analysis that both bare samples of ZnSe and af-GQDs exhibited compressional strain which could be ascribed to quantum confinement effect. However, the core-shell structure exhibited tensile strain that resulted due to lattice mismatch of the core and shell. This has strongly tuned the energy states within the structures by altering the band gap and further PL emission properties which is elucidated in the next sections of optical characterization.

The UV-vis spectrum of af-GQDs Fig. 5 a, there are two absorption peaks ⁓238nm and ⁓332 nm with a long tail extending into the visible region Fig. 5a. The π–π* transition absorption peak of benzene or aromatic sp2 domains can be attributed to the peak at 238 nm. An n–π* transition in the conjugated structure is revealed by the absorption peak at 330 nm [52]. The predicted Eg value of GQDs from the x-axis intercept of an extended line from the Tauc plot is about 4.5 eV Fig. (Sc) (See Supporting information). As a result, distinct amounts of energy levels may be obtained. Photo-excited electron hole pairs will undergo radiative providing band gap and emission for graphene quantum dots through those distinct energy levels [53]. The absorbance spectra of ZnSe Fig. 5b shows the broad peak ranging from 300nm to 700nm that ascribe the broad particle size distribution that is the characteristic feature of the QDs [54,55]. The broadness of the absorbance peak is reduced in the absorbance spectra of the ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell structure Fig. 5c shows the passivation of the defect states of ZnSe by the af-GQDs shell.

The band gaps of the QDs were determined by Tauc plots depicted in Fig. 5(d−f) using the Tauc equation [56].

the bands gaps for af-GQDs, ZnSe QDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell QDs are listed in Table S2. The band gaps of the bare samples (af-GQDs and ZnSe QDs) are considerably higher due to the quantum confinement effect. Whereas there is a decrease in band gap observed in core-shell sample due to increase in particle size. The distribution of surface related defect states in QD structures that arise due to the structural defects estimated through their micro strains calculated from XRD graphs, can be related to the notable exponential increase in absorption tail below the fundamental absorption band edge. The exponential behavior of absorption tail is described as Urbach- Matienssen rule given in Eq. (4) [57,58].

Urbach energy EU of the QDs was computed by plotting the ln(α) Vs energy(eV) shown in Fig 5. The Fig. 5(g-i) depicts the relation between the lnα and EU and the value of Urbach energy is estimated by taking the inverse of the slope of the linear portion of the curves and are listed in Table S3. Urbach energy measures the system's overall energetic disorder. At room temperature, EU ranges from 10 to 100 meV depending on the semiconductor [59]. The calculated Urbach energies at room temperature for the QDs are listed in Table 2 . The Urbach energy of the core-shell structure is approximately the difference of the Urbach energies of the ZnSe QDs and af-GQDs which indicates that the defects states of ZnSe are effectively passivated by the 2D amino functionalized honeycomb network of the af-GQDs. the position of valance band and conduction band spectra on the basis of the band gap were determined using simplified linear model of tight bonding model [60] (See supporting information Table S2). The calculated CB and VB positions confirms the formation of type-II ZnSe/af-GQDs core/shell QDs. The type-II core/shell QDs have staggered band alignment that cause the prominent shift in absorbance and emission spectra. The photoluminescent properties of the prepared QDs at room temperature and in temperature range 80K-300K are investigated to probe the defect related radiative and nonradiative recombination processes.

The room temperature (RT) photoluminescence spectra the QDs is shown in the Fig. 6 . The PL spectra of ZnSe QDs shows the broad emission consist of prominent peaks around 462nm, 468nm (blue emission) 590nm (orange emission), 646nm (red emission) and 701 nm (deep red emission). The emission peak around 462nm (2.68eV) is due to the free-exciton recombination. The peak around 468nm is due the bound exciton or the shallow defects [61]. It is well known that the green emission in ZnSe QDs is attributed to the zinc vacancy (VZn) [62]. The broad band emission peak that extends from 590 to 701nm (orange to deep red) are originated due to deep level defect (DLD) that are originated due to the point defects such as vacancies (Schottky) and interstitials (Frenkel) defects. Due to vacancies (V) and interstitials (I) different localized energy levels originated in the band gap and also combine to form donor acceptor pairs (DAPs). Zn interstitials (IZn) and Se vacancies (VSe) introduces the energy levels near the conduction band edge and act as donor sites. The Zn vacancies (VZn) and Se interstitials (ISe) introduces the energy levels near valance band edge and act as acceptor sites. The electron hole recombination at donor acceptor sites results in low energy emission [63,64]. The relative low intensity peaks around 799nm and 866nm in IR region are originated due the localized energy level in band gap that are originated due to impurities and imperfection [65].

The PL spectrum of prepared amino (NH2) functionalized graphene quantum dots shows the emission peaks in the visible region at 458nm (blue emission) and other at 542nm (green emission) and 572nm (yellow emission). The former is due to the intrinsic state emission and the latter is due to surface states that arises due to the functional group at the surface of the af-GQDs and the quantum size effect [66]. The obtained PL emission is more dependent upon the surface states than the size of the af-GQDs [67]. It is therefore we believed that C=C sp2 carbon domains and amino functionalized groups introduces different electronic states due to the bounding and anti-bounding of the molecular orbitals. As a result, a tuneable PL is obtained due to the zigzag effect of band-to-band transition around 458nm (π* to π due to C=C bond) and interstate to band transition around 542nm and 572nm (n-π) due to amino edge functionalization [68]. The PL spectrum of core-shell ZnSe/af-GQDs shows three prominent peaks at 458nm (blue emission) and other two at 596nm(orange)and 684nm(red). The weak peak at 458nm is due to the overlapped characteristic band gap of the ZnSe and af-GQDs that originate due to the π* - π transition of C=C band of af-GQDs and is shown as highlighted portion of the spectrum in Fig. Sd (see supporting information). The weak intensity of blue emission may be ascribed to lower energy excitation wavelength (457 nm). The peaks at 590nm and 684nm is due to the DLD. The steady state PL spectra of the ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell shows a prominent shift towards the lower wavelength (higher energy) due to the surface passivation of ZnSe QDs with af-GQDs [69]. The peak center of the PL spectra of the ZnSe is at 717nm while in ZnSe/af-GQDs the peak center is at 650nm. The af-GQDs shell is responsible to cover the defect state that may cause nonradiative luminescence. The broadness of the PL spectra is also reduced for ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell due to the reduction of defects states of the ZnSe.

Further temperature dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectroscopy (in the temperature range 80K to 300K) has been done to study the role of defect related emission band in carrier storage and escape processes. The temperature dependent spectra of the quantum dots are shown in the Fig. 7 (a−c). Various emission bands were observed to exhibit anomalous behavior with increase in temperature in all the samples. For bare ZnSe sample, the red and green emission intensity decrease with the increase in the temperature while the orange emission showed quenching effect up to 120K and then increase when the temperature further rises. The PL Intensity of the blue band increases up to 250K and then quenching effect is observed. For the bare af-GQDs sample, the red, yellow and blue emission intensities increase up to 150K and then show quenching effect. Whereas the green emission intensity in this sample showed a linear rise with temperature and did not exhibit quenching effect. In the core-shell sample, the red and blue emission band intensities increased up to 120k and then showed quenching effect. On the other hand, the green and orange emission band intensities were quenched with temperature rise thus exhibiting negative temperature dependance.

To study the effect of temperature on the peak energy shift of the samples, a comparison of deconvoluted Gaussian fitted PL spectra at RT (300k) and 80K is shown in the Fig. 8 (a−c). The deconvoluted Gaussian fitted RT spectra of ZnSe shows the weak blue and prominent green, orange and red bands. The green and red band are red shifted by 7nm and 4nm while the orange band is blue shifted by 10nm with respect to the spectra at RT. The percentage intensity of the ZnSe is increased at low temperature 80K due to decreases in thermally induced nonradiative recombination. On the other hand, the blue band quenched with decreased in temperature. The PL spectra of the af-GQDs at 300K shows the three prominent bands green, yellow, and red in the visible region while the spectra of the af-GQDs at 80K shows the additional orange band. The green and red band of the are blue shifted by 10nm and 20nm respectively. The yellow band is red shifted by 9nm and in case of 300K the orange band merged into yellow that becomes prominent at 80K. The ZnSe/af-GQDs contain green, orange, red and deep red emission bands all bands show red shift of 6nm, 4nm and 5nm and deep red band is 10nm blue shifted. The PL intensity of the ZnSe/af-GQDs core-shell structure increased with the decrease in temperature.

In order to study the effect of the temperature on the band gap of the structure the Temperature dependent peak energy shift is studied in detail by comparing the resolvable Gaussian fitted spectra of samples for entire temperature range (80K < T < 300K) as shown in Fig. 9 (e,f). For ZnSe QDs the peak position of the red and green band decreases towards lower energy (red shift) with increase in temperature and follows the Varshani model [62].

Where γ is the band narrowing coefficient and θD is the Debye temperature. The Varshani behavior arises due to both temperature dependent lattice expansion and electron lattice interaction [46]. The value of γ and θD for red band by fitting the experimental data are find out 79meV and 395K respectively. The calculated values are well agreement with literature [64].

The blue band of the ZnSe shows increase in energy (blue shift) with increase in temperature. The blue shift is due to the increase in the population of thermally excited free carriers. The energy of the orange band start increases from T > 120K and then decreases exponentially. All bands of af-GQDs show the increase in band gap position within the range 80K < T < 180K and then decrease for 180K < T < 250K and then remain unchanged for T > 250K. The blue and red bands of the ZnSe/af-GQDs Core shell structure show the decrease in energy with increase in temperature and also follow the Vershani model and the values γ and θD are for blue band are 196meV and 151K and for red band are 87meV and 271K. while the orange and green bands show the irregular behavior with increase in temperature.

Further to probe the effect of temperature on the emission intensity of the QDs the integrated PL intensity are plotted as a function of the reciprocal of temperature as shown in the Fig. 10 . The PL intensity of red emission arises due to DAP recombination and green emission of Zinc vacancy of the ZnSe QDs shows the normal thermal quenching effect with decrease in temperature. The quenching effect of PL at lower temperature is due to the decrease in thermally activated non radiative recombination. While the orange and blue emission shows the increase in intensity with increase in temperature then decrease with increase in temperature which is the Berthelot type of behavior. The origin of this abnormal Berthelot's type of behavior in semiconductors can be related to excited electron and holes that can be recombine radiatively an non radiatively to the conduction and valance band tail states. Normally, at lower temperature carrier diffusion to DLD is minimum due to lower electron and holes mobilities that results in increase in PL intensity and then with increase in temperature carrier hopping increases from radiative tail states and results in decrease in PL intensity. But it is possible that with increase in temperature that carrier in nonradiative tail sates excites to radiative tail states and PL intensity increases [62,65].

The integrated intensity of all bands of af-GQDs shows the increase intensity with increase in temperature up to some characteristic temperature and then PL intensity decreases with temperature increase. All bands of af-GQDs shows Berthelot type of behavior. The red and blue bands of ZnSe/af-GQDs shows the Berthelot type of behavior. The change in blue emission intensity has been shown in the highlighted portion of TDPL spectrum of the sample in Fig. Se(see supporting information) and orange, and green bands shows the normal quenching effect with increase in temperature. Thermally activated carrier models Arrhenius model Eq. (6) and Berthelot's model Eq. (7) are used to fit the experimental data of different bands and carrier escape and activation energies are calculated.

Where the Io is the intensity at 80K and f1 and f2 are the frequencies of carriers for radiative emission and for hopping between the states and Er and Eesc are the radiative and escape energies of the carriers and TB and EB are the Berthelot temperature and energy respectively. The experimental data is fitted using Eqs. (6) and (7) and shown by solid line in Fig. 10. The activation, hopping, escape energies and the free parameters used to simulate the fitting functions are listed in Table 2. It was observed that for ZnSe the activation energy of the carriers in the orange band is quite lower -42meV than the blue (74meV), green (97meV) and red (26meV). Its means that the luminescence of the orange band is increasing with increase in temperature. Similarly with increase in temperature the escape energy of the charge carriers for ZnSe is also lower than the blue, green and red band this would be due to the increase in the jump of charge carrier into defect sates as compared to the Zn and Se related vacancies. The activation energies for af-GQDs blue, green, yellow and red bands are -226meV, 79meV, 75meV and 332meV respectively. The activation energy of the red band is much higher than the other bands this is due to the decrease in deep level defects (DLD) states of af-GQDs with increase in temperature that enhance the luminescence in the visible region especially in the blue region. It is interesting to note that activation energy of the blue band of the ZnSe/af-GQDs Core-Shell structure is -53meV which is much lower than the activation energy of the green(16meV), orange(57meV) and red(17meV). The values of the activation energies of the core-shell structures are less than the ZnSe this due to the formation of shell around the ZnSe that cover the defect sates of the ZnSe core.

The decay time τ of the all-spectral components was estimated using the Sander -Cheng theoretical model Eq. 8 which was later reported by the Masalov et al. [70,71].

The relative PL- quantum yield (PLQY) of the prepared QDs were estimated using a comparative method and calculated PL-QY are 58%, 61% and 83% for ZnSe, af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs respectively. For the detail description of the calculation (See supporting information Table S3).

We herein, present a first report on a facile and eco-friendly synthesis approach to obtain the ZnSe/af-GQDs type-II core/shell QDs having a potential single emissive layer (SEL). The structural properties of the ZnSe/af-GQDs were investigated using XRD and vibrational modes of the core/shell QDs were studied via Raman analysis. The grain size and the strain of the prepared QDs were estimated by WH-plots the particle size of the ZnSe/af-GQDs is ⁓25nm. It was observed that the compressional strain converted to tensile strain in core/shell QDs indicating a soft interface between the core/shell QDs and a type-II band alignment. The chemical composition of the samples was analyzed using X-ray Photon spectroscopy (XPS). The morphology of the core/shell QDs was confirmed from Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The structural, chemical, and morphological characterizations confirmed the successful formation ZnSe/af-GQDs core/shell QDs. The optical properties of the QDs were investigated in detail using UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The thickness of surface defect states of the QDs was estimated by determining the Urbach tail energy model. The Urbach tail energy was found to decrease in core/shell structure which indicated that shell formation passivated the core effectively. The CB and VB edge positions were estimated using linear equation of tight binding model that confirmed the formation of type-II band alignment. The steady state PL emission properties of ZnSe/af-GQDs were investigated using 457nm excitation laser that exhibited a weak blue and strong green, yellow, orange, and red emission, whereas the full width half maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectra in ZnSe/af-GQDs was significantly reduced. In the visible band (458-750nm), we obtained highly efficient adjustment of PL emission spectral components. The functional groups connected to af-GQDs ascribed the soft-shell formation, and the soft core-shell contact greatly reduced Auger recombination and strain-induced defect states (AR). The temperature dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectroscopy of the samples is measured within the temperature range of 80-300k. For TDPL observations, the carrier activation and escape energies of radiative and non-radiative recombination were calculated using Arrhenius and Berthelot fitting functions. The radiative energies of the ZnSe/af-GQDs are found to decrease as compared to bare ZnSe QDs. The decay time τ for all spectral components were estimated using theoretical model. The relative PLQY of QDs were estimated using comparative method and calculated PLQY for ZnSe, af-GQDs and ZnSe/af-GQDs are 58%, 61% and 83%. The creation of a soft core-shell interface for ZnSe/af-GQDs is explained in detail. We believe that the ZnSe-/af-GQDs structures described in this paper have a lot of potential for LEDs and display systems that require multi-color components, such as white light emission. This work may pave the way for new and exciting ways to use af-GQDs in optoelectronic devices (Table 3 ).
